content_en:
The category of Da'if (weak) hadith encompasses a vast range of narrations whose chains of transmission or textual content fail to meet the criteria of authenticity established for Sahih and Hasan grades. Understanding the Da'if category — its causes, its subcategories, and the rules governing its use — is one of the most practically important skills for any student of Islamic knowledge, because weak hadiths appear in many of the most widely circulated religious texts and popular books of fada'il (virtues of acts).

Ibn al-Salah explains that hadith weakness can arise from two fundamental sources: a defect in the isnad (chain of transmission) or a defect in the matn (text). Defects in the isnad are by far the more common. They include: irsal (a missing Companion link); inqita' (a broken link anywhere in the chain); tadlis (a narrator concealing that they did not directly hear from their teacher); weak narrators (those who are liars, accused of lying, severely faulty in memory, or largely unknown); narrators known for excessive errors; and narrators whose narrations contradict more reliable transmitters.

The subcategories of Da'if are numerous and precisely named. A 'matruk' (abandoned) hadith is one whose sole narrator has been accused of fabrication or whose narrations are overwhelmingly contradicted. A 'munkar' (rejected) hadith is one in which a weak narrator's version contradicts what more reliable narrators have transmitted — the reliable version is called 'ma'ruf' (recognized). A 'mu'allal' (defective) hadith appears sound but contains a hidden defect discovered by expert analysis. A 'mudtarib' (confused) hadith has narrations of equal authority that contradict each other so significantly that no reconciliation is possible and no single version can be preferred. A 'maqlub' (overturned) hadith has its isnad inverted — names of narrators exchanged — whether by error or deliberate test.

The most severe subcategory is the 'mawdu' (fabricated) hadith — a statement falsely attributed to the Prophet ﷺ that he never said. The scholars of hadith considered the identification and dissemination of fabricated hadiths one of the most important services they could render to Islam, because acting on a fabricated hadith as if it were prophetic guidance is a serious distortion of the religion. Works dedicated to identifying fabricated hadiths — such as Ibn al-Jawzi's al-Mawdu'at — provide exhaustive lists of narrations that hadith specialists have identified as inventions.

The rules governing the use of Da'if hadiths have been a matter of ongoing scholarly debate. The dominant position is that Da'if hadiths may be cited in the context of fada'il al-a'mal (virtues of actions) — encouraging righteous deeds — but may not be cited to establish legal obligations, prohibitions, or theological beliefs. They must be disclosed as weak when cited, and their weakness must not be so severe as to border on fabrication. A severely weak hadith is treated by most scholars like a fabricated one: not to be cited even for encouragement, and certainly not to be acted upon.
